skill.name: prompt-architect
skill.description: A structured prompt engineering framework for creating, reviewing, and improving prompts for Claude, ChatGPT, Codex, and other AI systems.
skill.summary: Create, assess, and refine reusable AI prompts with structured guidance and anti-pattern checks.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 29 suspicious patterns, but contextual review found them to be false positives in Markdown prose, prompt examples, or Graphviz labels. No scripts, command execution, network activity, credential access, prompt injection attempt, or malicious intent were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts in Prompt Guidance
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts occur in documentation lines about prompt structure, examples, and workflow labels. No cryptographic functions, hashes, ciphers, keys, or security-sensitive implementations are present at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain natural-language prompt guidance or Graphviz labels, not executable cryptographic code. The scanned skill is documentation-only, so there is no weak algorithm use to exploit.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Alerts in Examples
low_findings.description: Static system-reconnaissance alerts occur in examples about formatting, empty results, prompt anti-patterns, and diagnostic questions. The lines do not instruct access to host details, files, environment variables, or system commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context shows ordinary prompt-writing examples and checklist text. I found no commands or instructions that collect system information.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Shell Backtick Alerts from Markdown Delimiters
low_findings.description: Ruby or shell backtick alerts are caused by Markdown code fences and an explanation of triple backtick delimiters. The skill does not execute shell commands or tell the assistant to execute user-controlled text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are Markdown formatting examples, including an opening fence and delimiter guidance. There is no shell, Ruby, subprocess, or command interpolation behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network Reconnaissance Alert in Reasoning Guidance
low_findings.description: The network-reconnaissance alert points to guidance about asking an AI model to explain reasoning. No URL, network tool, port scan, DNS lookup, or external request appears at the flagged line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a natural-language instruction for prompt design. I found no network operations or reconnaissance intent in the surrounding section.

content.value_statement: Reusable prompts often fail because goals, context, and output rules are unclear. This skill gives a structured review process for stronger AI prompts.
actual_capabilities: Evaluates prompts for intent, clarity, context, structure, and output requirements.
Refines prompts with clearer goals, constraints, delimiters, and quality checks.
Applies prompting techniques such as self-consistency, few-shot examples, and plan-and-solve.
Identifies common prompt anti-patterns, including vague instructions and conflicting requirements.
Adapts guidance for analytical, creative, coding, transformation, and question-answering tasks.
Provides a reusable workflow for testing, iterating, and documenting prompt improvements.
limitations: It does not run benchmarks or automatically measure model performance.
It cannot guarantee the same output quality across every model or provider.
It requires the user to supply task goals, audience, constraints, and examples.
It focuses on prompt design, not application architecture or model fine-tuning.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Prompt Templates
use_cases.description: Create reusable prompts with clear structure, examples, edge case handling, and output rules for shared team workflows.

use_cases.title: Improve Inconsistent AI Outputs
use_cases.description: Review a prompt that produces variable results and add better context, constraints, validation steps, and success criteria.

use_cases.title: Teach Prompt Engineering Practices
use_cases.description: Use the framework to explain prompt changes and help others recognize patterns across different task types.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Basic Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this prompt for clarity, missing context, and output ambiguity. Explain the top three problems, then provide a revised version. Prompt: [paste prompt]

prompt_templates.title: Create a Reusable Prompt Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reusable prompt template for [task]. Include role, objective, required context, constraints, edge cases, output format, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a High-Stakes Workflow Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this prompt for repeated use in [workflow]. Apply plan-and-solve, self-consistency, and explicit uncertainty handling. Preserve required constraints. Prompt: [paste prompt]

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Prompt Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these prompts as a library. Group issues by pattern, identify repeated anti-patterns, propose standard template rules, and prioritize fixes by operational impact.

output_examples.input: A vague request asking an AI model to analyze customer feedback.
output: The revised prompt defines the analysis goal, target audience, and categories to extract.
It adds handling for unclear feedback, duplicate comments, and missing sentiment.
It specifies a concise summary followed by prioritized themes and supporting examples.

output_examples.input: A team prompt that asks for a comprehensive but brief product report.
output: The review identifies a contradiction between depth and brevity.
The improved version separates a short executive summary from detailed findings.
It defines word limits, required sections, and evidence expectations.

output_examples.input: A coding prompt that lacks language version, error handling, and output expectations.
output: The refined prompt states the programming language, runtime, inputs, outputs, and constraints.
It asks for error cases, tests, and assumptions before final implementation guidance.
It includes a verification step against the original requirements.

best_practices: State the task, audience, constraints, and output format before adding examples.
Use clear delimiters to separate instructions, source material, and expected outputs.
Test prompts against edge cases and document changes that improve reliability.
anti_patterns: Asking the model to make something better without defining quality criteria.
Mixing instructions and source content without clear boundaries.
Adding many special cases before the core task is clear.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps create, review, and improve reusable prompts for AI assistants and language models.

faq.question: Can it improve prompts for Claude and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes general guidance and model-specific considerations for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, ChatGPT, and similar systems.

faq.question: Does it test prompts automatically?
faq.answer: No. It gives a review and refinement framework, but users must run their own tests.

faq.question: Is this only for advanced prompt engineers?
faq.answer: No. It includes basic review steps and more advanced techniques for complex workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with prompt libraries?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports template design, anti-pattern review, and documentation practices for shared prompt libraries.

faq.question: Does it generate application code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on prompt quality and may help write coding prompts, but it is not a code generator.
